evidence: Protocol actions defines its own compatibility aliases and constants (`ContractCompatibility`, `CompatibilityStable`, `CompatibilityDeprecated`, `CompatibilityRemoved`, `CompatibilityBackwardSafe`) and current-version helper constants in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go`.
StarTram error contracts redefines the same compatibility alias/constant set and its own versioned descriptor shape in `goseg/startram/errors.go`.
Each module independently owns a parallel contract descriptor surface, creating duplicated evolution paths for compatibility policy.
README explicitly treats both files as core contract surfaces (`goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and `goseg/startram/errors.go`) but does not describe a single canonical governance owner.
A policy evolution (deprecation/removal handling) would require dual-package edits and synchronized reasoning to avoid drift.
runtime seam files aggregate many unrelated collaborators into broad callback structs and adapter interfaces, and most exported operations are only lightly wrapped before use
multiple modules are required to pass a large runtime container (`dockerRuntime`, `Runtime`, `bootstrapRuntime`, `startupRuntime`) even when only a subset is used
transition plumbing in rectify/broadcast repeatedly layers wrappers around the same state-update pattern

README relies on a Runtime Contract Gate script path check for contract surface changes, but edge-case behavior of contract predicates is not asserted in the files under review (`IsUploadActionContractDeprecated`, `IsUploadActionContractActive`, `APIConnectionErrorContractForVersion`).

Both contract modules are boundary-facing and expose version-compatibility decisions; missing direct unit assertions increases risk that boundary semantics regress before gate catches script-level contract mismatches.
No explicit co-located test cases are visible in this batch’s contract files for removed/deprecated/empty-version transition behavior.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Add deterministic in-package tests for contract-version edge cases (introduced, deprecated, removed, and unset/invalid versions) to complement the path-based gate and prevent silent drift.

evidence: Repository-wide, infrastructure boundary modules repeatedly use adapter structs composed almost entirely of one-line pass-through methods (`runtimeContainerAdapter`, `runtimeUrbitAdapter`, `runtimeSnapshotAdapter`, and `startramRuntimeDefault*Ops`) before adding business logic, which increases indirection without adding new behavior.

Startup and connectivity flow is centralized in `main.go` across CLI parsing, startup composition, dual-server lifecycle, connectivity polling, kill-switch scheduling, and signal handling in one file and one run path, making it difficult to test or replace individual concerns.
goseg/importer/importer.go logs ACME/configure errors without returning in finalize flow (finalizeImportedPierReadiness:960-963), while sibling steps in the same workflow return wrapped errors.
prepareImportedPierEnvironment treats DeleteContainer/DeleteVolume failures as informational only (987-992), so caller cannot distinguish setup failures from non-fatal cleanup events.
startServerWithRuntime returns nil on the first ErrServerClosed event from one listener without confirming the other listener has not already returned a hard failure (main.go:359-366).

findings.summary: Protocol and StarTram each duplicate contract-compatibility type/constant shims, creating coupled boundary boilerplate across modules.
evidence: actions.go: `type ContractCompatibility = contracts.ContractCompatibility` and `CompatibilityStable`, `CompatibilityDeprecated`, `CompatibilityRemoved`, `CompatibilityBackwardSafe` constants are redefined as aliases.
errors.go: same alias type and same four compatibility constants are redefined again for StarTram-specific use.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared protocol-contract boundary module (or dedicated compatibility façade) that owns contract compatibility constants and expose canonical types once, then consume from both modules; remove duplicated re-export blocks.

findings.summary: Contract lifecycle checks are split into separate module-specific implementations, increasing risk of drift in compatibility semantics.
evidence: actions.go computes deprecation/active state in local helpers (`isActionContractDeprecated`, `isActionContractActive`) against `ContractMetadata`.

errors.go has independent exported helpers (`IsAPIConnectionErrorContractActive`, `IsAPIConnectionErrorContractDeprecated`, `APIConnectionErrorContractForVersion`) that separately evaluate version gates.
The two modules each maintain contract descriptors/metadata logic (`uploadActionContracts` + `apiConnectionErrorContract`) without a single shared contract registry.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared contract descriptor registry and generic lifecycle evaluator used by both modules so activation/removal/deprecation semantics remain consistent and centrally testable.

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime contracts are god-style bags, not domain-focused seams.
evidence: `dockerRuntime` in `runtime_seams.go` groups 10+ unrelated collaborator groups (context, file, container, image, config, urbit, wireguard, netdata, minio, command, volume, timer), creating a monolithic seam for many call sites.
`newDockerRuntime` populates all groups up front, even though a consumer like wireguard or urbit code only needs a narrow subset.
`wireguard.go` and `urbit.go` both accept the same runtime aggregate and read only feature-specific fields, so unrelated coupling is paid for even on narrow paths.
findings.suggestion: Split orchestration seams by domain boundary: create separate small runtime contracts for container/image, filesystem/artifacts, wireguard, urbit startup, and config snapshots. Construct each module (wireguard, urbit, collectors, subsystem) with only the subset it needs, and only keep a composed root runtime when explicit orchestration requires it.

findings.summary: Most runtime adapters are thin pass-throughs that add call depth without behavior.
evidence: `runtimeContainerAdapter`, `runtimeUrbitAdapter`, `runtimeSnapshotAdapter`, `startupBootstrapAdapter`, `startupImageAdapter`, and `startupLoadAdapter` in `runtime_seams.go` delegate directly to package functions with no translation.
`workflowRuntime` in `operations_runtime.go` similarly stores function fields that mostly forward directly into package-level actions and only checks nil, which repeats the same seam pattern.
startup wiring (`startupRuntime` and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`) carries many function callbacks primarily to pass-through behavior instead of expressing explicit contracts.
findings.suggestion: Remove adapter layers where they perform only delegation: replace with direct function fields on focused interfaces (or constructor params) and keep adapters only when behavior adaptation, policy, or translation is needed. This reduces chain depth and makes intent explicit at boundaries.

findings.summary: Transition dispatch and broadcast state mutation are duplicated through stacked wrappers.
evidence: `RectifyUrbit` and `RectifyUrbitWithContext` repeat near-identical switch arms for each `Startram` event type before calling `applyTransitionUpdate`.
`applyTransitionUpdate` just forwards into `broadcast.ApplyBroadcastTransition(true, transition)` without adding additional domain policy.

`broadcast/transitions.go` contains duplicated core flow between `ApplyBroadcastTransition` and `ApplyBroadcastMutation` (read state, mutate, `UpdateBroadcast`, optional notify) with only minor API surface differences.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate transition dispatch into a small registry-driven strategy and expose a single mutation pipeline helper so adding new transition kinds is data-driven instead of repeated switch branching and wrapper chaining.

findings.summary: Contract governance is duplicated across protocol/actions and startram packages, creating drift risk as compatibility policy evolves.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and `goseg/startram/errors.go` both re-declare equivalent compatibility aliases/constants and build contract descriptors in parallel.
Each file independently implements helper-style compatibility checks (`IsUploadActionContract*`, `IsAPIConnectionErrorContract*`) without shared policy ownership.
README identifies both as canonical contract surfaces, implying cross-module boundary coupling if policy changes are applied inconsistently.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared contract-governance module (or explicit API in `protocol/contracts`) for version policy descriptors and lifecycle helpers, and consume it from both modules so compatibility semantics remain single-sourced.

findings.summary: Contract/version edge behavior is not directly unit-tested in the listed boundary files, so compatibility regressions can slip past scripted checks.
evidence: Public helpers expose boundary behavior critical to compatibility (`IsUploadActionContractDeprecated`, `IsUploadActionContractActive`, `APIConnectionErrorContractForVersion`) but no immediate unit tests are defined in these modules to validate transition boundaries.
README documents CI gates for contract change verification, but does not show deterministic unit tests for these version predicate edge cases in the same batch scope.
Coverage of these boundary predicates is especially important because contract acceptance/compatibility decisions drive runtime and wire compatibility.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit unit test files for both `goseg/protocol/actions` and `goseg/startram` that assert compatibility predicate behavior across introduced/deprecated/removed/empty-version inputs and verify `UnsupportedActionError`/masked-error return semantics.

findings.summary: Runtime seam layers add abstraction depth but no business behavior, increasing cognitive and refactor cost.
evidence: `runtime_seams.go` defines multiple wrapper interfaces and adapter types whose methods mostly forward directly to package functions (`StartContainer`, `StopContainerByName`, `UrbitConf`, `LoadWireguard`, etc.) without enrichment.
`startramRuntimeDefaultContainerOps` and `startramRuntimeServiceOverrides` mirror the same forwarding pattern and are re-wrapped rather than consolidated.
`accesspoint.go` and `wifi.go` similarly expose extensive function-pointer-based injection for each dependency edge, again with minimal policy translation.
This creates a systemic seam pattern where dependency boundaries are verbose but not semantically meaningful.
findings.suggestion: Create a narrower, intentional runtime contract per subsystem that contains only behavior-meaningful calls (e.g., `ContainerControl`, `SnapshotOps`) and inject concrete collaborators directly; remove no-op adapter layers and replace one-line wrappers with focused facades where behavior actually changes. This should reduce call depth and make seam tests assert intent, not plumbing.

findings.summary: Main bootstrap mixes startup orchestration, transport lifecycle, connectivity policy, and host-mode side effects in one flow.
evidence: `main.go` combines CLI parsing (`parseStartupOptions`), bootstrap wiring (`bootstrapSubsystems`), HTTP/WS server lifecycle (`startServerWithRuntime`), network recovery policy (`C2cCheckWith`), and kill-switch reboot timing (`killSwitch`) in a single execution path.

`startServerWithRuntime` simultaneously starts both listeners, owns shutdown orchestration, and does cross-server error handling, so changes to one transport concern affect unrelated startup and kill-switch paths.
Tests in `main_test.go` only exercise that callbacks are wired, not that these lifecycle responsibilities are separated into independently testable units.
findings.suggestion: Split startup into explicit collaborators (e.g., `StartupCoordinator`, `ServerLifecycleManager`, `ConnectivitySupervisor`) with dedicated interfaces and data structs; keep `main.go` as composition-only. Move kill-switch logic into a small policy service driven by connectivity snapshots, and keep transport boot logic transport-specific.

findings.summary: Client connection state transitions keep inactive placeholders and skip normalization, producing mixed semantics in auth-state transitions.
evidence: `transitionClientStateLocked` has a fast-path for `targetAuth && !client.Active` that appends a client and returns without setting active state, creating a branch with different semantics from the normal transition path.
`enqueueFakeConnIfNilOrUnconnected` explicitly inserts placeholder `MuConn` values, and cleanup/transition logic then has to special-case inactive/nil connections, increasing state-machine ambiguity.
The result is a brittle mix of transport lifecycle, placeholder management, and auth-state transitions in the same function, which is hard to reason about and extend safely.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `ClientManager` state updates around a single explicit transition function that always normalizes `(connection, active, bucket)` as an invariant and remove placeholder insertion from auth-state transitions. Represent temporary pending sessions separately from active session buckets to keep transition logic coherent.

findings.summary: Imported pier post-processing ignores ACME fix failures and continues as success.
evidence: In finalizeImportedPierReadiness, when ctx.Fix is true, acme.Fix errors are only logged and not propagated (lines 960-963), so callers still get nil and mark readiness as successful.
The same function later returns wrapped errors on wireguard switch failures, indicating this omission is inconsistent within one function's error contract.
findings.suggestion: Propagate acme.Fix failure as a wrapped error (or classify it as a typed soft-fail), and explicitly choose whether import should abort or continue; add a test that asserts the function fails when acme.Fix returns an error in finalized import path.

findings.summary: Import environment cleanup errors are logged but not surfaced, masking real setup failures.
evidence: prepareImportedPierEnvironment swallows DeleteContainer/DeleteVolume errors and continues to CreateVolume/MkdirAll (987-997), so stale container/volume state can persist or be silently bypassed.
This block is in an error-returning setup function where adjacent calls (CreateUrbitConfig, AppendSysConfigPier, CreateVolume, os.MkdirAll) use explicit `return fmt.Errorf(...)` semantics.
findings.suggestion: Replace blind log-and-continue with explicit handling: propagate unexpected failures, explicitly allow and wrap expected no-op cases (e.g., already absent), and add regression tests for deletion failures with different sentinel errors.

findings.summary: HTTP/websocket startup can hide a failed listener behind a graceful shutdown from the other listener.
evidence: startServerWithRuntime returns nil on listener.ErrServerClosed, but only after the first listener event is received (359-366), without verifying no prior/future unexpected listener error occurred.
The tests assert listener failure is surfaced, but do not cover the reverse-order race where one listener exits ErrServerClosed while the other exits with non-nil error.
findings.suggestion: Track listener outcomes and only return nil when both listeners have exited with graceful shutdown (or context cancellation), otherwise return the first non-nil unexpected error; add a test that simulates one listener returning ErrServerClosed and the other returning a real error.

findings.summary: Session boundary is shared via package-level mutable state across auth modules, coupling behavior to process-wide globals.
evidence: `authsession/session.go` uses package-global auth/session boundary state and exposes mutators, and auth modules read those globals as implicit dependencies.
No explicit boundary/session object is threaded into call chains; cross-module behavior changes when one package sets shared state.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to pass an explicit session-boundary/state service into auth entrypoints (struct field or context), remove package mutators, and guard shared mutable maps with scoped synchronization if process-shared caches are required.

findings.summary: Nil request handling is inconsistent in token/session creation flow, creating avoidable panic paths.
evidence: `CreateToken` dereferences `r.Header` without a nil check in the request identity path.
Adjacent parsing helpers in the same package provide nil-safe behavior, so callers and tests can exercise mixed semantics unexpectedly.
findings.suggestion: Normalize request preprocessing in `tokens.go`: either require non-nil by contract with explicit early error at all callsites, or accept nil safely in one shared helper used by all token functions.

findings.summary: Container image/registry selection leaks special-case logic that bypasses the shared orchestration abstraction.
evidence: `registry.go` applies a hard-coded branch for `llama-api` that does not flow through the generic container-type resolution path.
Orchestration and orchestration consumers still dispatch behavior by raw string type matching, making each new container class require touching multiple switch points.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a typed container strategy map (e.g., interface/registry metadata struct with image resolver function) and remove one-off type conditionals in favor of declarative registrations.

findings.summary: High-risk modules in this batch lack direct tests for concurrency, command construction, and branch-specific container resolution.
evidence: No tests are present for accesspoint command-building and execution boundary conditions in the listed package set.
Auth-session/global-boundary and persistence paths are mutation-heavy but lack deterministic unit tests for concurrent callers, nil guards, and rollback behavior.
findings.suggestion: Add focused tests around shell command assembly and execution boundaries, session boundary mutation races, and registry-special-case resolution paths using deterministic fakes and failure injection.

findings.summary: Access-point command execution uses shell-string formatting, reducing cohesion and observability of side-effect boundaries.
evidence: Command strings are composed from runtime values and executed via `sh -c`, coupling parsing, escaping, and execution into one brittle unit.
Error paths are emitted as low-level shell failures rather than structured command-domain errors, making fault attribution and recovery harder.
findings.suggestion: Build commands with typed argument arrays and call `exec.Command` directly; keep shelling only at a single adapter layer and return domain-specific errors with sanitized command metadata.
